speaker: interviewer
text: Marion Speicher, S-P-E-I-C-H-E-R. This is roll number 6.

speaker: interviewer
text: Marion, you were telling us about your new work here in America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I tell you my new work, let me go back a little bit to Germany and Bremenhafen. I met a fellow on the street, a friend of mine, which also didn't admit of being-- to being Jewish. And he also didn't know whether anybody was there. But After a long while knowing him, he admitted to me that he's Jewish. I admitted to him that I am Jewish. After I was on the ship for a while, on the Polish ship, I took him in. There was another opening and he went with me. And we arrived together in the United States. We were quite close for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Mike-- Mike Rzepkowicz. R-Z-E-P-K-O-W-I-C-Z. He changed his name later to Mike Randon-- R-A-N-D-O-N. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, between my trips to New York, and meeting my wife, and meeting a few Jews, I started going up to New York and Manhattan. On 103rd Street, there was a hotel called Marseille, where a lot of people from Germany used to arrive. There was their first-- if they had family or whatever, they didn't have whoever brought them in, that was their first point of arrival. I used to meet a lot of people, and that's how slowly, slowly I got acclimated with Jewish people again. Because I really lived the life of a Polack. And if I hadn't met any Jews, probably I don't know what would have happened. I might have never come back to Judaism. But the more I met people, and then I met some people from my hometown, and we're friendly til today. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] life on ships was very good. We were sailing all over. I made two trips on one of the ships to Hawaiian Islands, which was beautiful. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to your hometown in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never went back to my hometown. I never went back to Poland, even. As a matter of fact, two years ago there was a big gathering in my hometown of people from here, and from Israel, and from all over the world because they put up a monument, a few monuments at the places where people-- mass graves. And also there is a monument at this place where my mother was killed. I never went back. Why I didn't go back, I don't know. I can't explain. But I never did. I want to go back, and then I don't want to go back. The town, I understand, is completely different. Because during the war, the homes, the Jewish homes, as soon as they took away the Jewish family, they used to take apart the whole home. They used to use the-- homes were mostly built out of wood, so they used to use it for firewood. So-- so the town is completely different. And my life in the United States-- I lived in Brooklyn for five years. We had a nice apartment. My son Howard was born there. And then after a while, after two years, we bought a house in Bayside, Queens, which is part of New York City. It's a suburb, really. And we had another son there in 1958 by the name of Ronald. And they both grew up there. And we lived there for 37 years in the same house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think that your experiences during the war-- what effect do you think they had on the way you lived your life and raised your children? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I try not to dwell on what happened during the war. As a matter of fact, I never mentioned or told my children my story. And that's why they're waiting for this tape, so they could hear my life story. I never told them. I never made any excuses for any of my shortcomings, which I probably don't have.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I never said anything to them like, I didn't have it, why should you have it? Or, I was in trouble, why should you have it so good? Things like that. I brought them up-- we brought them up, my wife and me, just like any normal family that really did not go through the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: They are well-adjusted. They have no problems. I myself don't really have any problems. I am very strong. I don't dwell on the things that happen. If I do, I think to myself. I don't tell anybody. And I can live with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever have dreams?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very seldom, very seldom. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember some dreams that I had during the war. And until today, I don't know if that was dreams or that was reality. And I really would like to find out, but I don't know how.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because those dreams were reoccurring time, after time, after time. It was only during the war, not after the war. And I can't tell whether it's real or not, whether these things really happened. But this is also fading away. You know, it's more than 50 years. How much can you remember? Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I survived, I think, by pure luck. There was nothing that I was smart, or I did something right, or I was in the right place at the right time. It was just pure luck. That's how I attribute it, is because you can't-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you can't attribute it to anything else, in my situation, anyway. Some people went on-- they got false papers, and-- and they lived through the war if they weren't caught in different cities, usually in big cities, working, and so on and so forth. But I was never that lucky. As a matter of fact, going back, way, way back in my high school, in Gymnasium, I had a friend, a very good friends, close friend that during Polish times-- Poland was mostly Catholic. He was a Lutheran. So I used to make fun out of him. But he's a German, he used to call me the Jew. But we were very friendly. When the Germans came in, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and yet-- and we were taking up German language in Gymnasium. And he was like a nail. He couldn't speak a word. When the Germans came in, there were two police departments. One was a Reiterzugpolizei, which was on horses. And one of those guys was some kind of relative of his, from Germany, because they must have been German nationality or something. And this kid, after a little while, the first few weeks I used to see him, I used to tell him, I'll give you my papers and you can run away. Nothing materialized. And a few weeks later when that policeman showed up, his relative, he became a Hitler Jugend. And once I came face to face with him, and I thought I'll die of fear. He didn't say a word, just passed me by. He was sent out to Germany for a special course. He came back. He spoke beautiful German. And he was wearing his uniform, and walking around with a little machine gun. And he was having fun. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was my really good friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that it's 50 years since the war ended, what would be your message that you would like to leave to your children and grandchildren? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: My message to my children and my grandchildren would be they should go on with their lives and not dwell on what happened. They should just remember. Because a Jew was put on this world for forever to suffer. Maybe now, with the existence of Israel, things may be a little different. And yet, what we see today in this country, anti-Semitism is on the rise. We have all kinds of factions-- factions of militias, and neo-Nazis, and Nazis, or whatever you want. And they always have the Jew in mind. The Jew is the banker, and the Jew controls the press, and the Jew controls Hollywood, and the Jew controls everything else.

speaker: interviewee
text: So God, evidently, did a good deed that made us chosen people. And we're suffering because of that. And there'll be no end to the Jewish suffering unless everybody will settle in Israel, in his own country. That's my opinion. And we don't feel-- I don't feel any anti-Semitism, because I don't go any such places where I could meet up with these people.

speaker: interviewee
text: I live a very sheltered life now. We live in Florida, like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very luxurious life, and no-- no worries, no problems. There's a little crime here and there, so we try not to go to certain neighborhoods. But in general, we live a very sheltered and very good life. Our children have it pretty good. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One of our-- my-- the older son lives in Boston. The younger one lives in New Jersey, in Livingston. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The older son has three children. He's got-- the oldest one is Joshua, then there's Benjamin, and there's a girl Tanya, which was named after my mother. The younger one has two children, a boy and a girl. The boy is older. His name is Cory. And the girl is not even a year old, and her name is Jenna. They have good homes. They're happy homes. They live a very conservative life, which may be influence of the home they grew up in. But we all know that when you marry, a lot depends on the wife. The boys are just the followers most of the time. And I'm very happy with them, except I'm not very happy I don't see them too often.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think sustained you during the war years?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war years, probably the will to live. Everybody wanted to survive. And it's an unfortunate thing to say, even-- even for myself to say it. What the Germans did to us, they dehumanized us to such a degree that we didn't behave like human beings any more during the war. Mothers used to leave their children. The children used to leave their parents. Everybody wanted to survive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We stopped being human beings. Even animals behave better. They won't leave their small ones. But what they did to us, there is no forgiving for another 1,000 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: There is no excuse for what they did. We lived like-- what can I tell you? What you saw on on the film, was nothing, because I was there up to the point where he took his people to [NON-ENGLISH]. I was there in Plashov and all these places. He represented it very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there's nothing to compare to what we went through in the ghettos, and the hiding places, and little spaces people used to spend two or three years without food, without water, and fear, constant fear. That was the worst. Like myself, I was always afraid of beating. They used to beat you so brutally that there's no saying what they could do to you. They could kill you. Once they killed you, it wasn't so bad. But the beating was so brutal that-- beyond compare. And everything else, the whole life was to no description. Cannot describe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I hope that people will not forget. Some people forgive very easily. Some people don't forget. And there's a lot of Nazis still living. They were living for the past 50 years a very happy life. Even today, in the there's a story of a very high SS officer that changed his name right after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he's 85 years old. He's a very highly successful professor-- humanitarian. And he worked with Himmler all the years. So how can you-- if you get friendly with a person that is today 70 years old, or 75, or 68, you don't know who he was and what he did during the war. And 90% of them won't admit.

speaker: interviewee
text: And where were all the killers? There were hundreds of thousands of them. There wasn't just a few assessment. There were hundreds and thousands of them all over, all over Europe. To the last minute, when they saw they were losing the war, when there was a month before the war, a week before the end of the war, they were still killing the Jews. Thank you for telling us your story. Thank you very much. Marion, would you like to introduce your wife now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. This is my wife, Bianca. I call her Bianca. They changed her name to Blanche, which I don't like and she doesn't like. Her name was born, name given Bianca, which is a beautiful name for a beautiful lady. We are married 44 years, and quite happy. She's a wonderful mother, very good wife. She went through a lot during the war. She can't speak about it, but I know part of her story. And she was very young, can't remember a lot of things. And like I said before, living in fear. She was underground for two years living and hiding, and in terrible conditions. So you don't remember too much. You don't want to remember. But fortunately, she survived with her whole family, mother, father, and brother that they all deceased, but they died in the United States already. And they had a few good years with her family. They saw some grandchildren, which is a great thing. Grandchildren is the biggest dividend that we have after this whole unfortunate thing that we went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you, Blanche.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Lena Speicher. A picture taken in Borislav, Poland, approximately 1939. Marion Speicher, picture taken in August of 1939 in Borislav. This is Marion Speicher. This picture was taken in Bremenhafen in Germany. The time of the picture was sometime-- I say October or November of 1945. This is a picture of my father, whose name was Herman Speicher. It was taken in-- in Borislav, sometime either in 1941 or '42 for identification purposes. The date of the photo, as I said, sometimes 1941 or '42. This is a picture of Marion Spakovsky at that time. Was taken in London in 1945, and is attached to a Polish seaman's book. That was the only identification I had. If you notice on that picture, I'm wearing a sweater, because I didn't own a shirt. That was right after the war. This is again Marion Speicher. That was a picture taken of me in New York in 1947. I used to look good. This is a picture of my wife's family, her parents. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My wife and myself are in there, too. Also, my wife's father, her mother, his brother, and my wife's brother. The children are Howard and Ronald. That's my-- our children. And my wife's brother's children are Polly and David. That was taken in Brooklyn approximately sometime in 1962. This is a family picture of-- in the center is Marion, myself, and Blanche. To my wife's right is our older son on Howard with his wife, Hangwei. To the right of myself is my younger son and his wife, Ronald and Karen. The photo was taken [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] maybe 1990. This is my son Ronald with his two children, Cory, and the newcomer, Jenna. The picture was taken in his home in Livingston, New Jersey, approximately maybe [PASUES] beginning of 1995. This is my oldest son Howard and his wife Hangwei. Children are Joshua the oldest, Benjamin, and Tonia, the youngest. Picture was taken in Boston sometime in 1993. This is a picture of my wife and myself, Bianca and Marion. Place is Livingston, New Jersey in 1993. We were going to a wedding. We're all spruced out. This is my younger son, Ronald, with his wife Karen and the baby, the oldest child of theirs, Cory. The picture's taken at their home, Livingston, New Jersey, in 1994.
